analogy: Think of "Rise of Popular Movements" through the chapter's own opening images: In this chapter… We have seen in the last chapter that the Congress recovered after 1971, but was not the same kind of party. The difference became clear in a series of events between 1973 and 1975 that brought new challenges to India’s democratic politics... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Rise of Popular Movements is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that in this chapter… We have seen in the last chapter that the Congress recovered after 1971, but was not the same kind of party.

The text explains that the difference became clear in a series of events between 1973 and 1975 that brought new challenges to India’s democratic politics and the institutional balance sought by the Constitution.

The text explains that these developments led to the imposition of ‘emergency’ in June 1975.

The text explains that normally, we would associate ‘emergency’ with war and aggression or with natural disaster.

The text explains that but this ‘emergency’ was imposed because of the perceived threat of internal disturbance.

The text explains that the Emergency ended as dramatically as it had begun, resulting in a defeat of the Congress in the Lok Sabha elections of 1977.

The text explains that in this chapter we focus on this crucial phase in the history of democracy in India and ask some questions that have remained controversial after all these years. • Why was Emergency imposed?

The text explains that was it necessary? • What did the imposition of Emergency mean in practice? • What were the consequences of Emergency on party politics? • What are the lessons of Emergency for Indian democracy?

The text explains that the editorial page of ‘Nai Dunia’ of 27 June 1975 was like any other day, except that the space for editorial was left blank.

The text explains that the editorial was “censored” using emergency powers.

The text explains that many other newspapers also carried such blank spaces–sometimes to protest against emergency.

The text explains that background to Emergency We have already studied the changes that were taking place in Indian politics since 1967.

The text explains that indira Gandhi had emerged as a towering leader with tremendous popularity.

The text explains that this was also the period when party competition became bitter and polarised.

The text explains that this period also witnessed tensions in the relationship between the government and the judiciary.

The text explains that the Supreme Court found many initiatives of the government to be violative of the Constitution.

The text explains that under intense pressure from students, supported by the opposition political parties, assembly elections were held in Gujarat in June 1975.

The text explains that thirdly, the Parliament amended the Constitution saying that it can abridge Fundamental Rights for giving effect to Directive Principles.

The text explains that many political workers who were not arrested in the first wave, went ‘underground’ and organised protests against the government.

The text explains that they were turned into political instruments of the ruling party and according to the Shah Commission Report, the administration and the police became vulnerable to political pressures.

The text explains that in north India, it was a massive electoral wave against the Congress.

The text explains that its critics felt that the Janata Party lacked direction, leadership, and a common programme.

The text explains that in an indirect manner the issue of welfare of the backward castes also began to dominate politics since 1977.

The text explains that year: 2005 Director: Sudhir Mishra Screenplay: Sudhir Mishra Ruchi Narain Shivkumar Subramaniam Cast: Kay Kay Menon, Shiney Ahuja, Chitrangada Singh party in power had absolute majority and yet, its leadership decided to suspend the democratic process.

The text explains that the 1977 elections for the first time saw the Opposition coming into power at the Centre.

The text explains that what would you consider as the reasons for this development?

The text explains that discuss the effects of Emergency on the following aspects of our polity. • Effects on civil liberties for citizens. • Impact on relationship between the Executive and Judiciary • Functioning of Mass Media • Working of the Police and Bureaucracy.

The text explains that in what way did the imposition of Emergency affect the party system in India?

The text explains that read the passage and answer the questions below: Indian democracy was never so close to a two-party system as it was during the 1977 elections.

The text explains that however, the next few years saw a complete change.

The text explains that soon after its defeat, the Indian National Congress split into two groups……….. … … …The Janata Party also went through major convulsions…..David Butler, Ashok Lahiri and Prannoy Roy. — Partha C hatterjee (a) What made the party system in India look like a two-party system in 1977?

The text explains that (b) Many more than two parties existed in 1977.

The text explains that why then are the authors describing this period as close to a two-party system?

The text explains that (c) What caused splits in Congress and the Janata parties?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Politics in India since Independence, Chapter 6: Rise of Popular Movements

schemes.name: In
schemes.point: In order to reduce expenditure the government froze the salaries of its employees. (NCERT Class 12 Politics in India since Independence Ch6 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 94 Politics in India since Independence and 96 Politics in India since Independence as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Rise of Popular Movements; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Rise of Popular Movements through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 94 Politics in India since Independence and 96 Politics in India since Independence to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
